critique.summary: The article introduces Nanobrew, a macOS package manager written in Zig, claiming it is significantly faster than Homebrew. It highlights features such as parallel dependency resolution, APFS clonefile usage, and a single static binary design to achieve high performance.
mainPoints: Nanobrew is a macOS package manager written in Zig.
It claims to be up to 7,000 times faster than Homebrew in some operations.
Uses APFS clonefile for zero disk cost installs.
Parallelizes downloads, extraction, relocation, and dependency resolution.
Implements native HTTP client and Mach-O binary reading for efficiency.
Content-addressed store with SHA256 keys enables skipping redundant downloads.
Distributed as a single static binary without Ruby or interpreter dependencies.
critique.truthfulness: The claims about Nanobrew's speed and architecture are plausible given the described technical approaches such as using APFS clonefile, parallel processing, and a native HTTP client written in Zig. However, there is no independent benchmarking or third-party verification cited to confirm the exact speed improvements over Homebrew. The specific speed comparisons and factors like '7,000x faster' appear to be based on internal tests, which may not generalize. The technical descriptions align with known macOS and Zig capabilities, but the lack of external sources or multiple perspectives limits full verification.
considerations: No independent benchmarks or third-party reviews are cited to verify speed claims.
Compatibility with the extensive Homebrew package ecosystem is not discussed.
Potential trade-offs in stability, security, or community support compared to Homebrew are not addressed.
The article does not mention limitations or scenarios where Nanobrew might not outperform Homebrew.
No information on how Nanobrew handles updates, security patches, or package maintenance.

comments_critique.summary: The discussion revolves around the Nanobrew project and its compatibility and performance relative to Homebrew. Commenters debate the feasibility of full compatibility without Ruby, the importance of speed versus compatibility, and share personal experiences with Homebrew's architecture and migration challenges.

comments.author: mikemcquaid
comments.text:
If it doesn’t ever execute Ruby: it cannot be compatible with Homebrew. “Compatible” is doing a bit of work here when it also means “implicitly relies on Homebrew’s CDN, CI, packaging infrastructure and maintainers who keep all this running”.

There’s a new vibe coded Homebrew frontend with partial compatibility and improved speed every few weeks.

Homebrew is working on an official Rust frontend that will actually have full compatibility. Hopefully this will help share effort across the wider ecosystem.
comments.analysis: This comment accurately highlights the technical dependency of Homebrew on Ruby and its infrastructure, providing a nuanced understanding of what compatibility entails.

comments.author: tzs
comments.text:
OT: speaking of Homebrew, I made an incorrect assumption about it that eventually led to some problems. It was me being stupid, but I bet others have made the same mistake but not yet hit problems. Hence this comment.

My mistake was when I upgraded from my 2017 iMac (Intel processor) to an Apple silicon Mac at the start of 2024 and migrated via Time Machine I did not do anything extra specifically for Homebrew. I just assumed that as things got updated via the normal periodic Homebrew updates I run it would start grabbing the Apple silicon binaries for binary things it installed.

It turn out that is wrong. They made Apple silicon Homebrew kind of independent of Intel Homebrew. Intel Homebrew uses /usr/local and Apple silicon Homebrew uses /opt/homebrew. This allows having both native and Intel Homebrew installed at the same time if you need both.

The correct way to migrate from an Intel Mac to an Apple silicon Mac is to install Apple silicon Homebrew on the new Mac, and then install all the packages you want. Intel Homebrew works fine on Apple silicon Macs so you can use the Intel Homebrew that migrated via Time Machine to make the package list to use with Apple silicon Homebrew (or you can make it on the old Mac).

I only noticed this because I was trying to build something from source using some libraries that were installed via Homebrew and running into problems. An LLM was helping figure this out and it was telling me I might have to manually symlink those libraries from where they were in /opt/homebrew to where the build process for the thing I was building expected to find them and I didn't have a /opt/homebrew. The libraries were somewhere in /usr/local. I then noticed those libraries were not for Apple silicon, checked other things installed view Homebrew and saw nothing was for Apple silicon, and realized I had the wrong Homebrew.
comments.analysis: This detailed personal experience explains a common migration pitfall with Homebrew on Apple silicon Macs, providing useful practical advice.

comments.author: drob518
comments.text: This feels like a solution looking for a problem. I have a couple hundred brew packages on my system and I’ve never sat there thinking “If this was only 2 seconds faster…” while doing an update. I’m sure the Homebrew folks could mine this for a few ideas of how to further optimize brew, but I don’t think I’ll be adopting it anytime soon. Compatibility is more important than speed in this case.
comments.analysis: The comment offers a pragmatic user perspective emphasizing compatibility over speed, which aligns with many users' priorities in package management.

comments.author: chuckadams
comments.text: This might be a good thing for homebrew to adopt for the download/install process, but if it doesn't include a ruby interpreter, I have a hard time seeing how it's going to be compatible with anything but searching and installing bottles.  I install most of my packages from a Brewfile, which itself is Ruby code.
comments.analysis: This comment correctly points out the importance of Ruby in Homebrew's operation, especially for complex package management tasks involving Brewfiles.

comments.author: ryandrake
comments.text:
What would be great is a Homebrew-compatible system that doesn't cut off support for older machines. I have a 3.8 GHz Quad core i5 iMac that still crushes, yet Homebrew has determined that I'm just too old and icky[1] to work with anymore. I had to move over to MacPorts, which is surprisingly nice, but I still miss brew.

Yea, I know. It's open source. They can do what they want. Still sucks.

1: https://docs.brew.sh/Support-Tiers
comments.analysis: The comment reflects a valid user concern about hardware support policies in Homebrew, providing context on alternative package managers.

comments.author: maxloh
comments.text:
How does this work? AFAIK Homebrew formulae are written in Ruby [0].

Do they use some kind of Ruby parser to parse formulae?

[0]: https://github.com/Homebrew/homebrew-core/blob/26-tahoe/Form...
comments.analysis: This comment raises a relevant technical question about how Nanobrew might handle Ruby-based formulae, indicating curiosity about implementation details.

comments.author: alsetmusic
comments.text: I'm not a Python dev, but I appreciate the motivation uv has inspired across other package managers. I tried another brew replacement called zerobrew last month. It installed packages to a different directory from homebrew, so I didn't actually test drive after seeing that. Regardless, I look forward to the competition pushing mainstream tools to improve their performance.
comments.analysis: The comment provides comparative insight about similar tools and expresses optimism about competition driving performance improvements.

comments.author: Onavo
comments.text:
The current version of brew has a flaw where the installer can't install isolated dependency trees in a sterile manner. If you have packages A, B, C, and D that all have updates, and assuming A,B,C depend on each other and come out to a total of say 1MB, and D is 1000MB, brew works in a MapReduce manner where it will attempt to finish downloading everything in parallel (even though the real bottleneck is D) before doing any installation.

Since the first 3 has no dependency on D, a better way would be to install them in parallel while D is still downloading.
comments.analysis: This comment offers a thoughtful critique of Homebrew's update process, suggesting a more efficient installation strategy.

comments.author: kassadin
comments.text:
Do you choose compatibility or speed?

nb info --cask codex-app

nb: formula '--cask' not found

nb: formula 'codex-app' not found
comments.analysis: This comment highlights practical compatibility issues with Nanobrew, illustrating limitations in handling certain formulae.

comments.author: themadsens
comments.text:
I naively assumed it would work on the already installed homebrew packages. No such luck.

After installing, 'nb list' and thus eg. 'nb outdated' will yield the empty list! I have absolutely no use for a competing homebrew installation that is mostly compatible ..
comments.analysis: The comment expresses user frustration with Nanobrew's inability to recognize existing Homebrew packages, emphasizing the importance of full compatibility.
